title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about learning a musical instrument. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Instrument Speaks
content.subtitle: An article about what learning a musical instrument can teach us.
content.text:
There is a particular kind of frustration known to anyone who has tried to learn a musical instrument as an adult. The fingers will not cooperate. The sound that emerges is nothing like the sound in your head. You can hear clearly what the music should be, and yet your body refuses to produce it. [31] This gap between imagined perfection and physical reality is, paradoxically, one of the most educationally valuable experiences available to an adult learner — precisely because it is so uncomfortable. [/31]

Cognitive scientists who study skill acquisition describe musical training as one of the most demanding tasks the human brain can undertake. Learning an instrument requires the simultaneous development of motor coordination, auditory discrimination, memory, and real-time error correction. [32] Brain imaging studies have found that practising a musical instrument increases the density of neural connections in the areas responsible for these functions, with the greatest changes found not in prodigies who begin as children but in adults who persist through initial frustration. [/32] The struggling adult learner, it turns out, may be doing more complex cognitive work than the child who takes to the piano naturally.

There is also the question of what music teaches beyond technique. Experienced music teachers commonly report that what their students really learn is how to learn. [33] The discipline of returning to the same passage dozens of times, identifying where it breaks down and why, adjusting, and trying again, builds a metacognitive skill — the ability to monitor and direct one's own learning — that transfers to other domains entirely. [/33] Students who persist with an instrument through the difficult early months tend to perform better academically, not because music makes them smarter, but because it trains them in a particular quality of attention.

The social dimension of music is equally significant. Playing in an ensemble — a community orchestra, a jazz quartet, a school band — requires a quality of listening that goes beyond technical accuracy. [34] You must hear not only your own part but the parts of everyone around you, adjusting your timing, volume, and tone in real time in response to what you hear. [/34] Musicians describe this as a form of heightened conversation, more demanding and more rewarding than most spoken exchanges.

For adults who give up during the early stages, the most common reason given is lack of time. But teachers and psychologists who study motivation argue that this explanation rarely tells the whole story. [35] What most learners are actually avoiding is the discomfort of incompetence — the profound unpleasantness of being bad at something you care about. [/35] Those who persist tend not to be the most talented but the most comfortable with that discomfort, which is itself a skill that practice builds.

The music, when it finally comes — when fingers and memory and ear align and something recognisable emerges — produces a satisfaction that those who have never experienced it struggle to describe. [36] It is not merely the pleasure of achievement. It is, in some sense, the pleasure of having become a slightly different person: someone who has learned, through patience and repetition, to make something beautiful out of nothing. [/36]

questions.question: The writer describes the gap between imagined and produced music as
options: A) a sign that adult learners are unsuited to learning instruments.
B) an experience that, despite its discomfort, has real educational value.
C) something that only affects learners who begin as adults.
D) a problem that technology could help to eliminate.
questions.explanation: The writer calls it 'one of the most educationally valuable experiences available to an adult learner — precisely because it is so uncomfortable', meaning it has value despite being unpleasant.

questions.question: Brain imaging studies found that the greatest neural changes from learning an instrument occur in
options: A) children who show natural musical talent from an early age.
B) professional musicians who have trained since childhood.
C) adults who continue practising despite initial difficulties.
D) learners who focus on auditory discrimination rather than motor skills.
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states 'the greatest changes found not in prodigies who begin as children but in adults who persist through initial frustration'.

questions.question: Music teachers report that the most important thing their students actually learn is
options: A) how to perform confidently in front of an audience.
B) how to understand and direct their own learning process.
C) how to read music accurately and quickly.
D) how to collaborate effectively with other musicians.
questions.explanation: Teachers say students learn 'how to learn' — specifically a 'metacognitive skill — the ability to monitor and direct one's own learning' — which transfers to other areas.

questions.question: Playing in an ensemble is described as particularly demanding because it requires
options: A) memorising complex pieces without the aid of sheet music.
B) adjusting one's own playing continuously in response to others.
C) achieving a level of technical accuracy that is impossible alone.
D) taking on the role of leader within the group.
questions.explanation: Players must 'adjust your timing, volume, and tone in real time in response to what you hear' from others — a continuous, responsive adjustment.

questions.question: According to the writer, adults who give up learning an instrument usually do so because
options: A) they genuinely do not have enough time to practise.
B) they find they have less natural talent than they expected.
C) they are unwilling to endure the discomfort of being unskilled at something they value.
D) they receive insufficient support and encouragement from teachers.
questions.explanation: The writer says 'What most learners are actually avoiding is the discomfort of incompetence — the profound unpleasantness of being bad at something you care about'.

questions.question: The writer suggests that the satisfaction of eventually playing music successfully comes from
options: A) the recognition it brings from other musicians.
B) having developed as a person through the process of learning.
C) proving that adult learners can match the ability of those who started younger.
D) understanding the technical complexity of what has been achieved.
questions.explanation: The writer says it is 'the pleasure of having become a slightly different person' through 'patience and repetition', emphasising personal growth as the source of satisfaction.
